perturbation.original_text: 10. Nature of Relationship. The parties expressly acknowledge and agree ---------------------- that Sabco is acting as an independent contractor. Each party is responsible for all taxes relating to its operation, including payroll taxes for its employees, and nothing in this Agreement is intended to create a relationship, express or implied, of employer-employee between Sabco and Prolong. Except as expressly authorized herein, neither party may contract for or otherwise obligate the other party without the party's prior express written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Nature of Relationship. The parties expressly acknowledge and agree ---------------------- that Sabco is acting as an independent contractor. Prolong will handle all taxes relating to Sabco's operation, including payroll taxes for Sabco's employees, and nothing in this Agreement is intended to create a relationship, express or implied, of employer-employee between Sabco and Prolong. Except as expressly authorized herein, neither party may contract for or otherwise obligate the other party without the party's prior express written consent.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that Prolong will handle Sabco's payroll taxes, the contract introduces ambiguity about the independent contractor status. Independent contractors are responsible for their own taxes. This change suggests an employer-employee relationship, contradicting the stated intent and potentially misclassifying Sabco, which affects tax obligations and liabilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the definition of 'employee' under the IRS code. Specifically, the modification implies that Prolong is responsible for withholding income taxes from Sabco's payments, treating Sabco's personnel as employees, which is inconsistent with the stated independent contractor relationship. 26 U.S.C. § 3401 defines employee as someone whose employer is required to withhold taxes, and by assuming that tax responsibility, Prolong blurs the line between contractor and employer.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 3401 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 3401 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Wages For purposes of this chapter, the term “ wages ” means all remuneration (other than fees paid to a public official) for services performed by an employee for his employer , including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash; except that such term shall not include remuneration paid— (1) for active service performed in a month for which such employee is entitled to the benefits of section 112 (relating to certain combat zone compensation of members of the Armed Forces of the United States) to the extent remuneration for such service is excludable from gross income under such section, (2) for agricultural labor (as defined in section 3121(g) ) unless the remuneration paid for such labor is wages (as defined in section 3121(a)), (3) for domestic service in a private home, local college club, or local chapter of a college fraternity or sorority, (4) for service not in the course of the employer ’s trade or business performed in any calendar quarter by an employee, unless the cash remuneration paid for such service is $50 or more and such service is performed by an individual who is regularly employed by such employer to perform such service. For purposes of this paragraph, an individual shall be deemed to be regularly employed by an employer during a calendar quarter only if— (A) on each of some 24 days during such quarter such individual performs for such employer for some portion of the day service not in the course of the employer ’s trade or business, or (B) such individual was regularly employed (as determined under subparagraph (A)) by such employer in the performance of such service during the preceding calendar quarter, (5) for services by a citizen or resident of
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the nature of an independent contractor relationship by stating that Prolong will handle Sabco's payroll taxes. This implies that Prolong is treating Sabco's employees as their own employees. 26 U.S.C. § 3401 defines wages as remuneration for services performed by an employee for their employer. The original contract explicitly states Sabco is an independent contractor. The altered clause introduces an employer-employee dynamic with respect to tax handling, thus contradicting the explicitly stated independent contractor relationship and IRS guidelines.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Driver Appearances. Sabco shall provide unto Prolong five aggregate ------------------ driver appearances (2) two-hours each at no fee. Prolong shall be responsible for all first class travel expenses relating to each driver appearance, i.e. hotel, airfare, meals, etc. Additional appearances by Sabco drivers shall be at the discounted rate of [ * ] ($[ * ]) Dollars per day per driver, [ * ]. Sabco shall further provide the services of each driver for one free day for commercial use (up to six hours each) for still photo shoot, TV and radio/commercial production. Prolong shall be responsible for all first class travel expenses relating to each driver appearance, i.e. hotel, airfare, meals, etc. All Sabco drivers, while under contract with Team SABCO, shall allow their likeness, voice, picture and signature to be used for commercial purposes endorsing Prolong at no additional costs during the term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Driver Appearances. Sabco shall provide unto Prolong five aggregate ------------------ driver appearances (2) two-hours each at no fee. Prolong shall only be responsible for coach travel expenses relating to each driver appearance. Additional appearances by Sabco drivers shall be at the standard rate of [ * ] ($[ * ]) Dollars per day per driver, [ * ]. Sabco shall further provide the services of each driver for one free day for commercial use (up to six hours each) for still photo shoot, TV and radio/commercial production. Prolong shall be responsible for economy travel expenses relating to each driver appearance. All Sabco drivers, while under contract with Team SABCO, shall allow their likeness, voice, picture and signature to be used for commercial purposes endorsing Prolong at standard costs during the term of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'first class travel' to 'coach travel' and 'no additional costs' to 'standard costs' impacts the valuation of services provided. The original language implied a higher value in-kind contribution from Sabco's drivers. This can affect the proper accounting treatment and whether the arrangement meets standards for fair market value, impacting compliance with IRS regulations related to sponsorships and endorsements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 274 - Disallowance of certain entertainment, etc., expenses
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the nature of the expenses for driver appearances and use of likeness, it affects the potential deductibility of expenses under 26 U.S.C. § 274. Section 274 outlines specific rules for deducting entertainment and business expenses. The changes introduce vagueness that makes determining the accurate value of the services and associated deductions more difficult, potentially leading to non-compliance with IRS regulations.

Disallowance of certain entertainment, etc., expenses (a) Entertainment, amusement, recreation, or qualified transportation fringes (1) In general No deduction otherwise allowable under this chapter shall be allowed
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 274 - Disallowance of certain entertainment, etc., expenses | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 274 - Disallowance of certain entertainment, etc., expenses U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Entertainment, amusement, recreation, or qualified transportation fringes (1) In general No deduction otherwise allowable under this chapter shall be allowed for any item— (A) Activity With respect to an activity which is of a type generally considered to constitute entertainment, amusement, or recreation, or (B) Facility With respect to a facility used in connection with an activity referred to in subparagraph (A). (2) Special rules For purposes of applying paragraph (1)— (A) Dues or fees to any social, athletic, or sporting club or organization shall be treated as items with respect to facilities. (B) An activity described in section 212 shall be treated as a trade or business. (3) Denial of deduction for club dues Notwithstanding the preceding provisions of this subsection, no deduction shall be allowed under this chapter for amounts paid or incurred for membership in any club organized for business, pleasure, recreation, or other social purpose. (4) Qualified transportation fringes No deduction shall be allowed under this chapter for the expense of any qualified transportation fringe (as defined in section 132(f)) provided to an employee of the taxpayer. (b) Gifts (1) Limitation No deduction shall be allowed under section 162 or section 212 for any expense for gifts made directly or indirectly to any individual to the extent that such expense, when added to prior expenses of the taxpayer for gifts made to such individual during the same taxable year, exceeds $25. For purposes of this section, the term “ gift ” means any item excludable from gross income of the recipient under section 102 which is not excludable from his gross income under any other provis
perturbation.justification: The change from 'first class travel' to 'coach travel' and 'no additional costs' to 'standard costs' introduces ambiguity and impacts the valuation of services, potentially affecting deductibility under 26 U.S.C. § 274. The original contract clearly outlined the specific types of expenses Prolong would cover, whereas the modified contract introduces vagueness. This vagueness affects determining the accurate value of the services and associated deductions making compliance with IRS regulations more difficult. Both Snippet 1 and Snippet 2 refer to 26 U.S.C. § 274, which disallows certain entertainment expenses. The modification raises concerns about proper accounting and compliance with fair market value standards, potentially leading to non-compliance with IRS regulations concerning sponsorships and endorsements.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Indemnification. --------------- A. Sabco shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless Prolong, its officers, directors, employees and representatives from and against any and all losses, claims, suits, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including attorney fees and count costs incurred by any of them arising out of: (i) Any breach of any warrant made by Sabco herein; (ii) Any acts done or words spoken (iii) Any claims by any persons arising from acts or omissions of any nature by Sabco, its employees or agents, including but not limited to, claims arising during the court of competition or practice in the performance of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Indemnification. --------------- A. Sabco shall indemnify Prolong from any losses caused by gross negligence, up to a maximum of $1000. Prolong's officers, directors, employees and representatives are not covered. This only applies to direct damages, and Sabco is not responsible for attorney fees or court costs. This indemnification only covers (i) Willful breach of contract, (ii) Only written acts approved by Sabco management (iii) Any direct claims arising from the competition.
perturbation.explanation: By significantly limiting the scope and amount of indemnification provided by Sabco, the modified clause undermines the fundamental purpose of an indemnification agreement. It introduces uncertainty about the extent to which Prolong is protected against potential liabilities arising from Sabco's actions and increases Prolong's risk exposure due to the nature of the relationship. The original text provided broad protection; the modified version severely restricts it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina General Statute § 22B-1 - Certain agreements against public policy
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification may violate N.C.G.S. § 22B-1 if the limited indemnification is deemed an attempt to exempt Sabco from liability for its own negligence or willful misconduct to a degree that is against public policy. While the statute primarily targets construction contracts, the principle of limiting liability for one's own fault can be broadly applicable. The original indemnification clause was meant to protect Prolong, but the modified version drastically weakens that protection, which could be construed as attempting to limit liability in a way that the law frowns upon. Given the potential risk associated with racing events, such a limitation could be problematic under North Carolina law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 22B-1 Chapter 22B. Contracts Against Public Policy. Article 1. Invalid Agreements. § 22B-1. Certain indemnity and defend agreements invalid. (a) Provisions in, or in connection with, a construction agreement or design professional agreement purporting to require a promisor to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee, the promisee's independent contractors, agents, employees, or indemnitees against liability for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property proximately caused by or resulting from the negligence, in whole or in part, of the promisee, its independent contractors, agents, employees, or indemnitees, is against public policy, void and unenforceable. Nothing contained in this subsection shall prevent or prohibit a contract, promise or agreement whereby a promisor shall indemnify or hold harmless any promisee or the promisee's independent contractors, agents, employees or indemnitees against liability for damages resulting from the sole negligence of the promisor, its agents or employees. (b) Provisions in, or in connection with, a construction agreement or design professional agreement purporting to require a promisor to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee, the promisee's independent contractors, agents, employees, indemnitees, or any other person or entity against losses, damages, or expenses are against public policy, void, and unenforceable unless the the fault of the promisor or its derivative parties is a proximate cause of the loss, damage, or expense indemnified. (c) Provisions in, or in connection with, a construction agreement that includes design professional services or a design professional agreement purporting to require a design professional to defend a promisee, the promisee's independent contractors, agents, or employees, the promisee's indemnitees, or any other person or entity against liability or claims for damages or expenses, including attorney's fees, proximately caused or allegedly caused by the professional neg
perturbation.justification: The modified indemnification clause significantly weakens the protection afforded to Prolong, potentially violating the principles outlined in N.C.G.S. § 22B-1, even though the statute is specifically for construction agreements, the underlying principle against limiting liability for one's own negligence could be broadly applicable. The original text provided broad indemnification; the modified version limits it to gross negligence up to $1000, excluding officers, directors, employees, and representatives, and attorney fees. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that provisions purporting to require a promisor to indemnify a promisee against liability for damages caused by the negligence of the promisee are against public policy and unenforceable. While the modified text doesn't explicitly indemnify Prolong against its own negligence, severely limiting the scope of Sabco's indemnification could be seen as an attempt to indirectly limit Sabco's liability for its own actions, thereby undermining the purpose of the indemnification clause and potentially violating the public policy principle behind the statute.
